meta.title: TB-DF-1: Tableau Desktop Connections, Visuals, and Core Calculations

questions.stem: A Tableau author needs to combine two Excel sheets that have the same columns and should stack row-by-row. Which data preparation operation should be used?

options.text: A join
A union
A blend
A hierarchy

questions.explanation: **B** is correct because a union appends rows from tables with matching schemas. **A** combines columns based on matching keys, while **C** and **D** solve different problems.

questions.stem: What is the main purpose of Data Interpreter in Tableau?

options.text: It schedules workbook subscriptions
It cleans common Excel formatting issues such as title rows and footers during import
It publishes extracts to Tableau Cloud automatically
It creates level of detail expressions

questions.explanation: **B** is correct because Data Interpreter helps clean spreadsheet-style formatting artifacts. **A**, **C**, and **D** are unrelated capabilities.

questions.stem: When would a pivot in the Data Source page be most appropriate?

options.text: When wide month columns need to become attribute-value rows
When two database tables need an inner join
When a workbook must be published
When a continuous field should become discrete

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because pivoting reshapes wide columns into a longer row-oriented structure. **B**, **C**, and **D** refer to different tasks.

questions.stem: A workbook needs customer attributes from one table and order facts from another using a shared customer key. Which operation is most appropriate?

options.text: A join on the shared key
A union of both tables
A manual sort
A dashboard action

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because joins combine columns from related tables using key logic. **B** appends rows instead, while **C** and **D** do not combine source tables.

questions.stem: Which source type can Tableau Desktop connect to directly?

options.text: Excel files
Only Tableau extracts
Only CSV files
Only cloud warehouses

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because Tableau can connect to Excel and many other sources. **B**, **C**, and **D** are too restrictive and do not reflect Tableau's connector support.

questions.stem: Which TWO statements about joins in Tableau are correct? (Select TWO)

options.text: A join combines columns from tables based on matching fields
Inner joins keep only records with matches on both sides
A join always stacks rows from one table under another
Joins can be used only with text files

questions.explanation: **A** and **B** are correct because joins combine related columns and inner joins keep matching rows only. **C** describes a union, and **D** is false because Tableau supports many source types.

questions.stem: What is a common reason to use a live connection instead of an extract?

options.text: To query the underlying source directly for the latest available data
To make the workbook unusable online
To disable filters
To remove all performance trade-offs

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because live connections query the underlying source directly. **B**, **C**, and **D** are inaccurate or exaggerated.

questions.stem: A JSON source contains nested attributes that need to be analyzed with relational tables later. What is the best first step inside Tableau Desktop?

options.text: Connect to the JSON source and inspect the logical fields Tableau exposes before modeling downstream views
Convert every field into a tooltip
Publish the workbook before reviewing the data source
Build a map immediately regardless of the fields

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because authors should first inspect how Tableau surfaces fields from the source before building analysis. **B**, **C**, and **D** skip the required preparation step.

questions.stem: What is the main purpose of a context filter in Tableau?

options.text: It defines a primary filter that other filters can depend on
It publishes a workbook
It converts measures to dimensions
It creates a union

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because context filters create a subset that later filters operate on. **B**, **C**, and **D** are unrelated features.

questions.stem: How does a set differ from a group in Tableau?

options.text: A set defines membership that can be dynamic or conditional, while a group combines members into named categories
A set is only for maps, while a group is only for tables
A set is always numeric, while a group is always date-based
There is no difference

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because sets manage in/out membership and can be driven by logic, while groups collapse members into ad hoc categories. **B**, **C**, and **D** are incorrect.

questions.stem: Why would an analyst create a hierarchy of `Region > State > City`?

options.text: To enable drill-down and roll-up navigation
To publish to Tableau Reader
To join two databases
To create a data extract automatically

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because hierarchies support drill paths across related geographic levels. **B**, **C**, and **D** do not describe hierarchy behavior.

questions.stem: A view should always show the top 10 products within the currently selected region. Which feature is most appropriate?

options.text: A dynamic top-N set or filter based on the current context
A static image export
A union in the Data Source page
Changing every measure to discrete

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because a dynamic top-N set or filter can respond to the current context and keep the ranking aligned to the selected region. **B**, **C**, and **D** will not solve the requirement.

questions.stem: What does sorting a dimension field change in a worksheet?

options.text: The order in which the members appear
Whether the workbook is published
Which data source is used
The Tableau project permissions

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because sorting changes display order. **B**, **C**, and **D** are workbook administration tasks, not worksheet sorting behavior.

questions.stem: Which TWO actions are valid ways to segment members for analysis in Tableau? (Select TWO)

options.text: Create a named group of selected members
Create a set using a condition or top-N rule
Replace the data source with a screenshot
Delete the measure values shelf permanently

questions.explanation: **A** and **B** are correct because groups and sets are both segmentation tools. **C** destroys analytic utility, and **D** is unrelated.

questions.stem: A filter should be easy for dashboard users to change without editing the workbook. Which option is best?

options.text: Show a quick filter control
Convert the field into a caption
Move the field to Tooltip only
Hide the field from the Data pane

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because quick filters expose interactive controls to end users. **B**, **C**, and **D** do not provide the same filter interaction.

questions.stem: What is the main benefit of a context filter before a computationally expensive top-N filter?

options.text: It can reduce the data considered by the later top-N logic
It guarantees zero query cost
It disables all other filters
It converts live data into an extract automatically

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because context filters define the subset used by later filtering steps. **B**, **C**, and **D** overstate or misstate what context filters do.

questions.stem: What is a `.twbx` file used for in Tableau?

options.text: A packaged workbook that bundles the workbook with local data resources
A file that stores only project permissions
A workbook version history archive on the server
A model evaluation report

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because a packaged workbook bundles the workbook and supporting local files for easier sharing. **B**, **C**, and **D** are different artifacts.

questions.stem: Why publish a data source separately from a workbook?

options.text: To let multiple workbooks reuse a governed source definition
To prevent anyone from connecting to data
To disable extracts
To turn measures into dimensions

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because published data sources support reuse and governance. **B**, **C**, and **D** are not the purpose of separate publication.

questions.stem: What does a subscription do in Tableau Cloud or Tableau Server?

options.text: Schedules email delivery of a view or dashboard
Creates a join between tables
Converts a workbook to JSON
Grants site administrator privileges

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because subscriptions send scheduled snapshots or links to users. **B**, **C**, and **D** describe unrelated features.

questions.stem: A team needs to share a dashboard with offline users who do not have server access. Which option is most appropriate?

options.text: Distribute a packaged workbook for Tableau Reader when the use case fits
Require every user to edit calculated fields manually
Convert the dashboard into a context filter
Use only a live connection to a private database

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because Tableau Reader can open compatible packaged workbooks offline. **B**, **C**, and **D** do not satisfy the offline sharing requirement.

questions.stem: Which TWO statements about permissions are generally true in Tableau environments? (Select TWO)

options.text: Project and content permissions affect what users can view or edit
Effective permissions matter more than a single isolated rule
Permissions are controlled by chart type only
Publishing automatically gives every user edit rights

questions.explanation: **A** and **B** are correct because access results from effective permissions across project/content settings. **C** and **D** are false.

questions.stem: Which export option is best when a user wants a static image of a dashboard for a slide deck?

options.text: PNG image export
A union
A context filter
A level of detail expression

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because PNG export provides a static image suitable for slides. **B**, **C**, and **D** are not export formats.

questions.stem: What is the most important governance reason to publish a workbook into the correct project?

options.text: The project can carry appropriate permission and content-management settings
It changes every chart into a map
It removes the need for data source credentials
It guarantees perfect performance

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because project placement affects access control and organization. **B**, **C**, and **D** are unrelated or overstated.

questions.stem: In Tableau, how are dimensions typically used in a view?

options.text: They usually segment or label the data
They can never be filtered
They are always continuous green pills
They store workbook permissions

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because dimensions commonly define categories, headers, and partitions in a view. **B**, **C**, and **D** are incorrect.

questions.stem: What is the visual difference between discrete and continuous fields in Tableau?

options.text: Discrete fields create headers, while continuous fields typically create axes
Discrete fields disable sorting
Continuous fields cannot be filtered
There is no difference

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because discrete fields behave as headers and continuous fields typically generate axes. **B**, **C**, and **D** are false.

questions.stem: Why might an analyst choose an extract instead of a live connection?

options.text: To use a snapshot or potentially improve performance for some use cases
To remove all need for refreshes forever
To guarantee real-time source data
To disable calculations

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because extracts can support performance and distribution scenarios. **B**, **C**, and **D** overstate or misstate extract behavior.

questions.stem: Which shelf on the Marks card controls whether text labels appear on marks?

options.text: Label
Pages
Filters
Columns

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because the Label shelf controls mark labeling. **B**, **C**, and **D** serve different worksheet functions.

questions.stem: Which TWO statements about measures in Tableau are correct? (Select TWO)

options.text: Measures are often aggregated in views by default
Measures commonly represent quantitative values such as sales or profit
Measures can never be used in filters
Measures are always discrete blue pills

questions.explanation: **A** and **B** are correct because measures are quantitative fields that are often aggregated by default. **C** and **D** are false generalizations.

questions.stem: What does changing the aggregation from `SUM` to `AVG` do?

options.text: It changes how Tableau summarizes the measure in the current view
It republishes the workbook
It changes a join into a union
It converts a dimension into a hierarchy

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because aggregation settings control how a measure is summarized in the view. **B**, **C**, and **D** are unrelated actions.

questions.stem: A field appears green in the view and creates a numeric axis. What does that most likely indicate?

options.text: It is being used as a continuous field
It is a published permission rule
It is a hidden worksheet
It is an input table

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because green pills usually indicate continuous fields that create axes. **B**, **C**, and **D** are unrelated concepts.

questions.stem: Which chart type is most suitable for showing change in a measure over time?

options.text: Line chart
Treemap
Packed bubbles without time
Highlight table only

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because line charts are a standard choice for time-series trends. **B**, **C**, and **D** usually communicate different analytical patterns.

questions.stem: Which view is often best for showing correlation between two quantitative measures?

options.text: Scatter plot
Stacked bar chart of one category only
Text table with no measures
A hierarchy control

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because scatter plots position two quantitative measures against each other to reveal relationships. **B**, **C**, and **D** are less suitable.

questions.stem: What is a common use case for a highlight table?

options.text: Showing magnitude patterns across rows and columns with color encoding
Building a cross-database join
Publishing subscriptions
Cleaning spreadsheet headers

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because highlight tables use color to emphasize patterns across a matrix of values. **B**, **C**, and **D** are unrelated.

questions.stem: When is a dual-axis chart most appropriate?

options.text: When two measures need to be compared in the same view, often with different mark types or scales that are handled carefully
When a workbook must be certified
When data needs to be unioned
When all fields are dimensions

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because dual-axis charts combine two measures in one view and require careful design. **B**, **C**, and **D** are unrelated.

questions.stem: Which TWO chart choices commonly fit geographic analysis in Tableau? (Select TWO)

options.text: Symbol maps
Filled maps
Union views
Join charts

questions.explanation: **A** and **B** are correct because Tableau supports both symbol and filled maps for geographic analysis. **C** and **D** are not standard chart types.

questions.stem: Which Marks card property controls the hue used for marks?

options.text: Color
Detail
Tooltip
Rows

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because the Color shelf controls mark hue and related formatting. **B**, **C**, and **D** control different aspects of the view.

questions.stem: Why should an analyst be cautious with packed bubbles or treemaps for exact comparisons?

options.text: Area-based encodings are harder to compare precisely than aligned lengths
They cannot display dimensions
They are the only option for quantitative data
They disable tooltips

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because people estimate aligned lengths more accurately than area. **B**, **C**, and **D** are false claims.

questions.stem: What is a calculated field in Tableau?

options.text: A user-defined expression that creates a new field from existing data
A permission rule on a project
A type of workbook export only
A static image annotation

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because calculated fields derive new values using formulas. **B**, **C**, and **D** are unrelated.

questions.stem: Which expression type calculates a value at a declared level such as customer or region regardless of the current view granularity?

options.text: A level of detail expression such as `FIXED`
A PNG export
A union
A worksheet title

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because LOD expressions define the aggregation level explicitly. **B**, **C**, and **D** are not calculation types.

questions.stem: What does a quick table calculation like running total do?

options.text: Applies a common table-calculation pattern without writing the full formula manually
Publishes the workbook to the server
Creates a data source filter automatically
Replaces all dimensions with measures

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because quick table calculations provide built-in analytic patterns such as running totals. **B**, **C**, and **D** are incorrect.

questions.stem: Why can `FIXED` LOD results differ from a simple aggregate in the current view?

options.text: Because the LOD can compute at a different declared granularity than the visible marks
Because Tableau ignores the source data
Because LOD expressions are not aggregated
Because FIXED always returns text

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because LOD expressions can decouple the computation level from the displayed level. **B**, **C**, and **D** are false.

questions.stem: Which TWO functions are commonly associated with table calculations rather than basic row-level string cleaning? (Select TWO)

options.text: `RUNNING_SUM`
`WINDOW_AVG`
`LEFT`
`UPPER`

questions.explanation: **A** and **B** are correct because they operate across a table partition. **C** and **D** are useful string functions, but they are not table calculations.

questions.stem: What is the purpose of an `IF` or `CASE` calculation in Tableau?

options.text: To return different results based on logical conditions
To create a union between tables
To publish extracts
To manage project permissions

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because conditional calculations branch based on rules. **B**, **C**, and **D** are unrelated.

questions.stem: What does disaggregating data usually change in a Tableau view?

options.text: It can show underlying records instead of summarized marks
It deletes the data source
It prevents filters from working
It makes every field continuous

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because disaggregation exposes less-summarized marks or records. **B**, **C**, and **D** are false.

questions.stem: An analyst needs each state's sales as a percent of the overall national total in the current table. Which approach best fits?

options.text: Use a percent-of-total table calculation or an equivalent calculation pattern
Replace the measure with a tooltip only
Create a union
Convert the workbook into Tableau Reader

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because percent-of-total is a standard table-calculation use case. **B**, **C**, and **D** do not produce the required analytic result.
